section.title: Philip in Samaria and the Ethiopian Eunuch
section.sectionRationale: The stoning of Stephen unleashes a persecution that scatters the Jerusalem church, and the very violence meant to crush the gospel becomes the wind that carries it outward. Philip, one of the seven, preaches Christ in Samaria, the old rival of the Jews, and the despised Samaritans believe. Then an angel and the Spirit send Philip to a desert road to meet an African court official reading Isaiah, and the gospel reaches to the ends of the earth in a single chariot.
section.thesis: The scattering of the church cannot stop the word but spreads it, so that in one chapter the gospel crosses the ancient wall between Jew and Samaritan and reaches an African official at the edge of the known world, proving that the risen Christ is gathering a people from every nation.
section.frameNote: Four movements: persecution scatters the church out of Jerusalem; Philip proclaims Christ in Samaria and Simon the sorcerer is exposed; the apostles come and the Samaritans receive the Spirit; and Philip meets the Ethiopian eunuch, opens Isaiah to him, and baptizes him.

units.label: Persecution scatters the church
units.note: the gospel driven out of Jerusalem

verses.text: And Saul was there, giving approval to Stephen’s death. On that day a great persecution broke out against the church in Jerusalem, and all except the apostles were scattered throughout Judea and Samaria.
God-fearing men buried Stephen and mourned deeply over him.
But Saul began to destroy the church. Going from house to house, he dragged off men and women and put them in prison.

units.label: Philip proclaims Christ in Samaria
units.note: Samaria receives the word, and even Simon believes

verses.text: Those who had been scattered preached the word wherever they went.
Philip went down to a city in Samaria and proclaimed the Christ to them.
The crowds all paid close attention to Philip’s message and to the signs they saw him perform.
With loud shrieks, unclean spirits came out of many who were possessed, and many of the paralyzed and lame were healed.
So there was great joy in that city.
Prior to that time, a man named Simon had practiced sorcery in the city and astounded the people of Samaria. He claimed to be someone great,
and all the people, from the least to the greatest, heeded his words and said, “This man is the divine power called the Great Power.”
They paid close attention to him because he had astounded them for a long time with his sorcery.
But when they believed Philip as he preached the gospel of the kingdom of God and the name of Jesus Christ, they were baptized, both men and women.
Even Simon himself believed and was baptized. He followed Philip closely and was astounded by the great signs and miracles he observed.

units.label: The apostles come and the Spirit is given
units.note: the Samaritans receive the Spirit, and Simon is rebuked

verses.text: When the apostles in Jerusalem heard that Samaria had received the word of God, they sent Peter and John to them.
On their arrival, they prayed for them to receive the Holy Spirit.
For the Holy Spirit had not yet fallen upon any of them; they had simply been baptized into the name of the Lord Jesus.
Then Peter and John laid their hands on them, and they received the {{pneuma|Holy Spirit}}.
When Simon saw that the Spirit was given through the laying on of the apostles’ hands, he offered them money.
“Give me this power as well,” he said, “so that everyone on whom I lay my hands may receive the Holy Spirit.”
But Peter replied, “May your silver perish with you, because you thought you could buy the gift of God with money!
You have no part or share in our ministry, because your heart is not right before God.
Repent, therefore, of your wickedness, and pray to the Lord. Perhaps He will forgive you for the intent of your heart.
For I see that you are poisoned by bitterness and captive to iniquity.”
Then Simon answered, “Pray to the Lord for me, so that nothing you have said may happen to me.”
And after Peter and John had testified and spoken the word of the Lord, they returned to Jerusalem, preaching the gospel in many of the Samaritan villages.

units.label: Philip and the Ethiopian eunuch
units.note: the gospel reaches an African official at the ends of the earth

verses.text: Now an angel of the Lord said to Philip, “Get up and go south to the desert road that goes down from Jerusalem to Gaza.”
So he started out, and on his way he met an Ethiopian {{eunouchos|eunuch}}, a court official in charge of the entire treasury of Candace, queen of the Ethiopians. He had gone to Jerusalem to worship,
and on his return was sitting in his chariot reading Isaiah the prophet.
The Spirit said to Philip, “Go over to that chariot and stay by it.”
So Philip ran up and heard the man reading Isaiah the prophet. “Do you understand what you are reading?” Philip asked.
“How can I,” he said, “unless someone guides me?” And he invited Philip to come up and sit with him.
The eunuch was reading this passage of Scripture: “He was led like a sheep to the slaughter, and as a lamb before the shearer is silent, so He did not open His mouth.
In His humiliation He was deprived of justice. Who can recount His descendants? For His life was removed from the earth.”
“Tell me,” said the eunuch, “who is the prophet talking about, himself or someone else?”
Then Philip began with this very Scripture and told him the {{euangelizo|good news}} about Jesus.
As they traveled along the road and came to some water, the eunuch said, “Look, here is water! What is there to prevent me from being baptized?”
And he gave orders to stop the chariot. Then both Philip and the eunuch went down into the water, and Philip {{baptizo|baptized}} him.
When they came up out of the water, the Spirit of the Lord carried Philip away, and the eunuch saw him no more, but went on his way rejoicing.
But Philip appeared at Azotus and traveled through that region, preaching the gospel in all the towns until he came to Caesarea.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. Here it is the Holy Spirit, promised by the risen Christ and poured out at Pentecost. In Samaria the same Spirit who fell on the Jerusalem believers is given to the Samaritans, publicly binding them into the one people of God.

eunouchos.gloss: A eunuch, often a trusted official in a royal court. As a foreigner and a physically marked man, he stood on the margins of the temple, yet Isaiah 56 had promised eunuchs and foreigners a name and a place among God's people, a promise the gospel now fulfils in him.

euangelizo.gloss: To announce good news, to preach the gospel. Philip opens Isaiah 53 and preaches the good news about Jesus, showing that the suffering servant led like a lamb to the slaughter is the Christ crucified and risen for sinners.

baptizo.gloss: To immerse, to baptize. The eunuch's request, what is there to prevent me, and his immersion in the water mark him openly as a disciple of Jesus, received into the church without regard to his nation or his status.

basic.context: Stephen has just been killed, and a fierce persecution scatters the Jerusalem believers, though the apostles stay. What looks like disaster becomes the engine of mission: the scattered believers preach wherever they go. Philip goes to Samaria, a people the Jews despised, and they believe. Then God sends him to a desert road to meet an Ethiopian official, and the gospel reaches an African at the edge of the map. The key questions are: why must the church be scattered, why do the Samaritans receive the Spirit only when the apostles come, and what does it mean that this foreign eunuch is welcomed in?
basic.hermeneutics: Read this chapter as the map of Acts 1:8 unfolding: from Jerusalem, into Judea and Samaria, and toward the ends of the earth. The Samaritan episode is a hinge, so the delay of the Spirit until Peter and John arrive is best read as God visibly uniting a once-divided people to the apostolic church, not as a rule that conversion and the Spirit normally come apart. The eunuch story shows how personal, Scripture-centred evangelism works.
basic.originalLanguages: The Samaritans receive the pneuma, the Holy Spirit, the same gift given at Pentecost. Philip meets an eunouchos, a court official and outsider, and tells him the good news, euangelizo, from Isaiah. The man asks to be baptized, baptizo, and is received openly as a disciple. The words trace the movement of the one gospel across old boundaries.
basic.typology: The Isaiah passage the eunuch reads, the lamb led to the slaughter who does not open His mouth, is fulfilled in Jesus, the suffering servant. The eunuch himself fulfils Isaiah 56, where God promises the foreigner and the eunuch a place and a name among His people. Philip's snatching away recalls the prophets carried by the Spirit.
basic.christ: Philip preaches Christ in Samaria and Christ from Isaiah 53 to the eunuch. The whole chapter is about proclaiming the name of Jesus. The servant who was humbled and deprived of justice, whose life was removed from the earth, is the crucified and risen Lord, and the good news about Him is what turns strangers into worshippers who go on their way rejoicing.
basic.god: God is sovereign over the scattering and the mission. He directs Philip by an angel and by the Spirit, arranges the meeting on the desert road, and gives His Spirit to the Samaritans. He shows no partiality, opening His people to Samaritan and African alike, and He exposes and warns Simon, whose heart is not right.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 1:8 sets the plan the chapter follows. Isaiah 53:7-8 is the very text the eunuch reads. Isaiah 56:3-8 promises the foreigner and eunuch a place. John 4 shows Jesus Himself reaching Samaria first.
basic.oneStory: This is the gospel breaking out of Jerusalem. The wall between Jew and Samaritan falls, and an African official carries the news home, a living picture of the promise that in Abraham's seed all nations would be blessed. The scattering that seemed to end the church actually launched it toward the ends of the earth.

context.literary: The chapter turns Stephen's death into the pivot of the whole book. Luke follows the program of Acts 1:8, moving the story from Jerusalem into Samaria and then toward the ends of the earth. It is told in two panels, Philip in Samaria and Philip on the Gaza road, framed by the scattering that begins it and Philip's arrival in Caesarea that ends it.
context.historical: Samaritans and Jews shared a heritage but were bitter rivals over worship and Scripture, so a Jewish believer preaching Christ there is a striking crossing of lines. The eunuch was a high official of the Nubian kingdom to the south of Egypt, a God-fearer who had travelled to Jerusalem to worship, reading a costly scroll of Isaiah on his long journey home.
context.canonical: Acts 8 is the first great outward movement of the church. It fulfils Jesus' word about Samaria and prepares for the Gentile mission that follows with Cornelius. Isaiah 53, quoted here, becomes the church's foundational reading of the cross, and Isaiah 56's welcome of the eunuch and the foreigner is shown coming true.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Luke shows how the risen Christ, by His Spirit, drives the gospel out of Jerusalem across the deepest human divisions, so that Samaritans and an African official are gathered in, and to model Spirit-led, Scripture-centred witness through Philip.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The dramatic events, the angel, the snatching away of Philip, the delayed giving of the Spirit, describe how God acted at this hinge of redemptive history. What is prescriptive is the pattern of preaching Christ from the Scriptures, welcoming believers of every nation, and the call to repentance that Peter presses on Simon.

debates.question: Why did the Samaritans receive the Holy Spirit only when Peter and John came and laid hands on them, rather than when they first believed?

views.label: A unique, transitional sign uniting Samaria to the apostolic church
views.case: The strongest case reads this as a one-time event at a hinge in history: God deliberately withheld the visible gift until the Jerusalem apostles came, so the old Jew and Samaritan hostility could not split the church into rival movements. The Samaritans are publicly bound into the one people of God under apostolic authority, and Luke elsewhere treats faith, baptism, and the Spirit as normally belonging together.

views.label: A pattern of a second, subsequent reception of the Spirit
views.case: Some read a gap between conversion and a distinct later reception of the Spirit, taken as a model for a second blessing after believing. This rightly notices the real interval in the text, but it must reckon with the exceptional, transitional setting and with passages where the Spirit is received at conversion.

debates.bearing: The first reading guards the unity of the church and the normal togetherness of faith, baptism, and the Spirit, while honouring that God sovereignly ordered this once-for-all moment to keep Samaritan and Jewish believers one.

study.originalLanguages: The pneuma, the Holy Spirit, falls on the Samaritans through the apostles, the same gift given at Pentecost, marking them as full members of God's people. On the desert road Philip meets an eunouchos, a court official and an outsider by nation and by body, and he tells him the good news, euangelizo, opening Isaiah 53 to show that the silent lamb is Jesus. The man asks to be baptized, baptizo, and is received without hesitation, a stranger made a brother.

typology.type: The lamb led to the slaughter, Isaiah 53:7-8
typology.fulfillment: Jesus the suffering servant, silent before His accusers, put to death and raised, whom Philip preaches as the good news to the eunuch.

typology.type: The eunuch and the foreigner welcomed, Isaiah 56:3-8
typology.fulfillment: The Ethiopian official, once barred from the assembly, is baptized and sent home rejoicing, a firstfruits of the nations coming in.
The old division healed as the despised Samaritans are gathered into the one church under the apostles, fulfilling Jesus' mission through Samaria.

study.christ: Christ is the content of every sermon here. Philip proclaims the Christ to Samaria, and on the road he begins with Isaiah 53 and tells the eunuch the good news about Jesus. The servant deprived of justice, whose life was taken from the earth, is the crucified Lord, and His resurrection is why the reader can rise from the water rejoicing. The gospel that crosses every boundary in this chapter is simply the announcement of His name.
study.god: God rules the whole account. He turns persecution into mission, directs Philip by angel and Spirit, times the desert meeting to the very verse the eunuch is reading, and pours out His Spirit on Samaria. He shows no partiality, welcoming Samaritan and African alike, and He is holy, exposing Simon's greed and calling him to repent. Salvation from first to last is His initiative.

crossReferences.note: You will be My witnesses in Jerusalem, in all Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth, the plan this chapter begins to fulfil.
The suffering servant led like a lamb to the slaughter, the passage the eunuch reads and Philip applies to Jesus.
God promises the foreigner and the eunuch a name and a place in His house, fulfilled as the Ethiopian is baptized.
Jesus Himself brings salvation to Samaritans, preparing for the wider harvest Philip reaps here.

study.oneStory: The gospel now bursts the walls of Jerusalem. Samaritans long estranged are received, and an African official reads Isaiah, meets the risen Christ in its pages, and carries the news home. Every ancient barrier of nation, worship, and status gives way as the promise to Abraham, that in his seed all peoples would be blessed, begins to reach the ends of the earth.

sermon.title: Personal Evangelism (Acts 8:26-40)
sermon.why: Begg walks through Philip's desert encounter with the Ethiopian eunuch, showing God as the director of evangelism who arranges the meeting, prepares the seeker, and saves through the plain preaching of Christ from Isaiah, exactly the climax of this study.

quiz.q: What did the persecution after Stephen's death actually accomplish?

options.text: It scattered the believers, who preached the word wherever they went, spreading the gospel outward.
options.note: Luke shows the scattering carrying the gospel into Judea and Samaria, fulfilling Acts 1:8.

options.text: It drove the apostles out of Jerusalem to begin the mission.
options.note: The mission did advance, but the text says all except the apostles were scattered; the apostles stayed in Jerusalem.

options.text: It silenced the church for a season until things calmed down.
options.note: The opposite happened; far from being silenced, the scattered believers preached everywhere.

options.text: It ended the growth of the church in that region.
options.note: The church grew and spread; the persecution served the gospel rather than stopping it.

quiz.q: Why is it significant that Philip preached to the Samaritans?

options.text: It crossed a deep, old hostility between Jews and Samaritans, showing the gospel gathers a once-divided people into one church.
options.note: The Samaritan mission is a hinge, healing an ancient division under the apostles.

options.text: It reached a nearby region so the gospel could spread more efficiently.
options.note: True geographically, but it understates the theological weight of crossing the Jew and Samaritan divide.

options.text: It proved the Samaritans had always secretly believed the same faith.
options.note: The point is not hidden agreement but that former rivals were now brought together in Christ.

options.text: It showed the gospel was only ever meant for the descendants of Israel.
options.note: The chapter shows the reverse, the gospel reaching beyond Israel to Samaritan and African alike.

quiz.q: How is the delay of the Spirit until Peter and John arrive best understood?

options.text: As a unique, transitional moment where God visibly bound the Samaritans into the one apostolic church.
options.note: This reading fits the hinge setting and guards the unity of a once-divided people.

options.text: As a fixed rule that the Spirit is always received in a second stage after believing.
options.note: This over-reads one exceptional event as a universal pattern, against passages where the Spirit comes at conversion.

options.text: As a sign that the Samaritans' first faith was not yet real faith.
options.note: Luke says Samaria received the word of God; their faith was genuine, not counterfeit.

options.text: As God honouring the apostles' role at this particular turning point in the church's history.
options.note: True and important, but it needs the fuller point that God was uniting Samaria to the whole church, not merely honouring the apostles.

quiz.q: What was wrong with Simon's request to buy the power to give the Spirit?

options.text: He tried to purchase the gift of God with money, revealing a heart not right before God.
options.note: Peter names both the sin, buying God's gift, and its root, a wrong heart.

options.text: He wanted spiritual power for himself rather than to serve others.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but the sharpest issue is treating God's free gift as something for sale.

options.text: He had not been baptized and so had no right to ask.
options.note: Simon had in fact believed and been baptized; the problem was his heart, not a missing rite.

options.text: He was never interested in the things of God at all.
options.note: He had followed Philip and was astounded by the signs; his error was corrupt motive, not total indifference.

quiz.q: What does Philip preach to the Ethiopian eunuch from Isaiah?

options.text: The good news about Jesus, the suffering servant led like a lamb to the slaughter.
options.note: Philip begins with Isaiah 53 and tells him the good news about Jesus.

options.text: That the Scriptures are difficult and need a teacher to explain them.
options.note: The eunuch did need a guide, but Philip's actual message was Christ crucified and risen, not merely the need for interpretation.

options.text: A set of moral rules the eunuch must keep to be accepted by God.
options.note: Philip preaches the person of Jesus, not a ladder of rules; the man is received by faith and baptism.

options.text: That his foreign birth barred him from being part of God's people.
options.note: The reverse is true; the eunuch is welcomed and baptized, fulfilling Isaiah's promise to the foreigner.

questions: The believers preached wherever the scattering sent them. When life has uprooted or unsettled you, how might God be using even that to spread His word through you?
Philip went to Samaria, a people his own culture despised. Who are the people you would find it hardest to bring the gospel to, and what would it look like to go to them?
Simon wanted the power of God without a heart that was right before God. Where might you be drawn to the benefits of faith while resisting the surrender it asks?
God arranged for Philip to meet one seeking man on a desert road. How does it change your view of ordinary encounters to know God may have prepared them?
The eunuch went on his way rejoicing after hearing Christ from Isaiah and being baptized. What would it look like for your grasp of the gospel to lead you into that kind of joy?

translationNotes.note: The BSB does not include a verse 37 in the running text, moving from verse 36 to verse 38, because the earliest and best manuscripts lack it. A footnote records that the later Textus Receptus adds, And Philip said, If you believe with all your heart, you may be baptized. The eunuch replied, I believe that Jesus Christ is the Son of God. This study follows the BSB and carries the omission, noting the added words are a genuine and orthodox confession but not part of the earliest text.
Candace is not a personal name but a royal title, like Pharaoh, for the queen mother of the kingdom of the Nubians to the south of Egypt; the BSB footnote gives the Greek as Kandake.

The quotation follows the Greek of Isaiah 53:7-8. The phrase who can recount His descendants and For His life was removed from the earth reflect the Septuagint wording that Philip and Luke are citing.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 8. Verse 37 is absent from the BSB running text and is carried as omitted, with a translation note. Footnote markers, poem and line-break objects were skipped when building verse text; poem line text was included. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon verified this session: Alistair Begg, Personal Evangelism, Acts 8:26-40, on truthforlife.org.
